%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Master the reference collection of trigonometric identities in Section B.8-6, understand their structural origins, and apply them confidently in exam problems.

Section B.8-6 collects the most frequently used trigonometric identities into one compact reference. These include product-to-sum formulas, sum-to-product formulas, power-reduction identities, and the important combination rule \(a\cos x + b\sin x = C\cos(x + \theta)\). Rather than memorizing each formula in isolation, you will learn to see the structural pattern behind each identity — why it holds, what it transforms, and where it appears in signal analysis and exam questions. After this section, you will be able to recognize which identity to apply given a product or sum of trig functions, and convert between equivalent forms quickly.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three structural families of trig identities from B.8-6: product-to-sum, power reduction, and amplitude-phase combination. The red callout flags the most common exam trap.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777219532928-8134.png)

## Core Idea

The identities in B.8-6 all share one structural purpose: **they convert inconvenient forms into convenient ones**. Products of trig functions become sums (easier to integrate). Powers of trig functions become linear combinations of lower-frequency terms (easier to analyze spectrally). And a mixture of sine and cosine at the same frequency collapses into a single cosine with a phase shift.

The most exam-critical identity is the **amplitude-phase combination**:

$$a\cos x + b\sin x = C\cos(x + \theta), \quad C = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2},\quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)$$

Think of \(a\) and \(b\) as the legs of a right triangle — \(C\) is the hypotenuse, and \(\theta\) is the angle. The most common exam trap is a **sign error in \(\theta\)**: the formula uses \(-b/a\), not \(+b/a\). Always verify the sign before writing your final answer.

### EXAM TIP

For product-to-sum identities, the factor of \(\frac{1}{2}\) (or \(\frac{1}{4}\) for cubed terms) is frequently dropped under time pressure — write it explicitly every time.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Product-to-sum identities convert trig products into sums, enabling integration and spectral analysis.
- Power-reduction identities express \(\cos^3 x\) and \(\sin^3 x\) as linear combinations of harmonics.
- The combination \(a\cos x + b\sin x = C\cos(x+\theta)\) collapses two terms into one; watch the sign of \(\theta\).

*With these trigonometric tools in hand, the next section (B.8-7) turns to common derivative formulas — the calculus counterpart that completes the mathematical toolkit for signal analysis.*
